content:
8 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

A post with its image, published straight from a chat, without touching a single backend form. Building it took an afternoon. The interesting boundary only showed up with the first photo.
A press release needs to go on the website as a post, with an image and a correct photo credit. Always the same motions: paste the text, find the image, download it, upload it again, type in the credit — exactly the kind of work that makes you wonder why you are still doing it yourself.
Since WordPress 6.9 there is an answer: the Abilities API, and on top of it the MCP Adapter from the Core AI initiative. Together they let you expose functions of your own installation as MCP tools that an AI client calls directly. This article describes what actually works — and where the design of the protocol draws a line you cannot configure your way around.
I’m Michael Seemann, a senior full-stack developer. This article is a small by-product: the website of a theatre support association, which turned out to be a good place to try MCP under real conditions.
What the adapter gives you — and what it doesn’t
An ability is a function with a contract: a unique name, typed input and output schemas, a permission_callback for authorisation, and an execute_callback doing the actual work. Once registered, it is callable from PHP, from JavaScript, and over the REST API. The MCP Adapter then translates such abilities into MCP tools.
In PHP it looks like this — trimmed to the essentials:
wp_register_ability( 'editorial/create-post', [
'label' => 'Create post',
'description' => 'Creates a post and publishes it.',
'input_schema' => [
'type' => 'object',
'properties' => [
'title' => [ 'type' => 'string' ],
'content' => [ 'type' => 'string' ],
'featured_image_id' => [ 'type' => 'integer' ],
],
'required' => [ 'title', 'content' ],
],
'permission_callback' => static function () {
return current_user_can( 'publish_posts' );
},
'execute_callback' => static function ( array $input ) {
if ( $existing_id = post_with_same_title_today( $input['title'] ) ) {
return new WP_Error( 'duplicate', 'Already exists.', [ 'post_id' => $existing_id ] );
}
$post_id = wp_insert_post( [
'post_type' => 'post',
'post_status' => 'publish',
'post_title' => $input['title'],
'post_content' => $input['content'],
], true );
if ( is_wp_error( $post_id ) ) {
return $post_id;
}
if ( ! empty( $input['featured_image_id'] ) ) {
set_post_thumbnail( $post_id, (int) $input['featured_image_id'] );
}
return [
'post_id' => $post_id,
'permalink' => get_permalink( $post_id ),
'status' => 'publish',
'site_url' => home_url( '/' ),
];
},
] );That’s all there is to it. The duplicate check, setting the featured image, the terse return value — all of it runs in PHP, on the server, with the permissions of the authenticated user. What the AI client ever sees is the input schema and the result.
The adapter itself is a plugin you install like any other — except you don’t get it from the official directory but from the project’s GitHub releases. Two consequences worth knowing up front: it does not update itself, and it currently sits at version 0.5.0, well short of a stable release.
The first call after installing returned exactly this:
{"abilities":[]}That is the right answer. The adapter brings the protocol plumbing — transport, server registration, three generic meta tools — and no domain logic whatsoever. WordPress itself ships three abilities, all read-only, and by default they aren’t even visible over MCP. If you want to offer something, you write it yourself.
For editorial work that came down to a handful of tools on a dedicated MCP server that exposes this list and nothing else:
create-post · update-post
create-media · update-media · get-media · list-mediaWhat’s missing from the list matters just as much: no delete tool. An agent allowed to publish does not also need to be allowed to remove.
How the client reaches the server
Between client and installation sits a proxy: @automattic/mcp-wordpress-remote, started locally via npx. It authenticates with an application password — a revocable access token WordPress issues per user, which you can withdraw individually without changing anyone's password. In the common MCP clients the entry looks the same:
{
"mcpServers": {
"my-site-production": {
"command": "npx",
"args": ["-y", "@automattic/mcp-wordpress-remote@latest"],
"env": {
"WP_API_URL": "https://example.org/wp-json/editorial/my-server",
"WP_API_USERNAME": "editorial-mcp",
"WP_API_PASSWORD": "xxxx xxxx xxxx xxxx xxxx xxxx"
}
}
}
}Two notes on that. The user behind it should be a dedicated one with as few capabilities as possible — not the administrator account you work with yourself; the permission_callback in every ability checks precisely that user's rights. And each stage gets its own entry. There is no stage parameter in the abilities: an ability always runs in the instance it is installed in. Which is exactly why the target address shows up in the return value later on.
Why write your own abilities instead of using the REST API
You might object that WordPress has had a write interface for years — and for posts and media, that’s true. Three reasons still argue for your own abilities.
Scope. The REST API is a general-purpose interface: every post type, every user, every setting. A dedicated server offers six tools and nothing else. What doesn’t exist can’t be called by accident.
Rules. The execute_callback runs server-side and may do more than store. It normalises values, checks for duplicates, derives computed fields. That logic belongs where the domain already lives — not in the caller, and certainly not in a language model.
The return value. Instead of a full object with forty fields, you get back exactly what’s needed next:
{
"post_id": 471,
"permalink": "https://…/blog/…/",
"status": "publish",
"site_url": "https://…"
}That site_url is not dead weight. Anyone running a local, a staging and a production instance side by side will eventually publish to the wrong one. Since the tools publish immediately and there is no delete tool, the target instance is the single most important piece of the response. The same call chain returned the staging domain once and the production domain the next time — demonstrable, not merely hoped for.
A second point from the same corner: a repeated call with an identical title and date does not create a second post, it returns the existing ID. An agent that sees an error likes to try again. Without duplicate protection, the post ends up on the site twice.
Media: sideload instead of upload
Up to here everything is text, and text fits through the MCP channel. With the first photo that changes.
The obvious design was to pass an image as Base64 in the call. That’s what articles about multi-modal MCP servers suggest too, with the caveat that it suits files under a megabyte. The caveat is right; the reasoning behind it is not. Those articles are worried about network overhead and the server’s memory.
The real problem sits elsewhere. Every tool call passes through the model’s context — and everything the model sends, it must first generate, character by character. A real featured image, 1086 × 1448 pixels, 2.4 MB, comes to roughly 3.2 million characters as Base64. Scaling down doesn’t save you:
Size File Base
641200 px203 KB~278,000 characters
800 px119 KB~163,000 characters
500 px52 KB~72,000 characters
Even the 500-pixel version would be a call with 72,000 characters — for a featured image the size of a postage stamp. And the commonly recommended fix, splitting the image into chunks, solves a different problem: it relieves the server’s memory, not the model’s context. Twenty calls move the same total volume, with session handling on top. I like to call that token burning: you’re paying a language model to retype an image.
What does work is the reference. create-media takes a URL, and the server fetches the file itself — bypassing the model entirely:
create-media
url: https://…/press/venue-exterior-2023.jpg
alt: The opera house seen from the square
caption: Opera house, main entrance
photo_credit: Jane DoeSixty characters instead of three million, and the bytes never touch the control channel.
The use case that justifies it
That makes create-media narrower than planned — and more valuable than the mere transport suggests. Because the value isn't in moving the file, it's in the credits.
Press pages offer photos for download, and the photographer’s name sits somewhere: in the filename, in the caption, in the fine print. Anyone doing this by hand downloads, uploads again, and reliably forgets the credit line. For anyone publishing someone else’s press photos, that is not a cosmetic flaw.
Over MCP it’s a single instruction: read the press page, extract image URLs and credits, one create-media per image with alt text, caption and photo credit. Complete metadata, no manual work — and the alt text comes along for free, which otherwise nobody ever fills in either.
One warning: press originals are large. A high-resolution photo pushed the sideload into a timeout on a small instance, because WordPress computes all intermediate sizes after downloading. If you build a size limit into such an ability, measure it against your weakest environment.
What about images on your own machine
That leaves the case with no URL: the photo on your phone. I have not found a satisfying answer for it, and I’ve come to think that’s correct.
A colleague ran into the same wall a project earlier with Canva. Local file uploads fail there too; there too the documented path is to upload through the interface and reference the result by ID afterwards. Arriving at the same place twice, independently, says less about a shortcoming of either interface than about a property of the design.
What helps is a read tool. list-media returns the most recently uploaded images with ID, filename and metadata. The human does the visual part in a familiar window — pick a file, upload it — and the agent finds the result and builds the post around it. Nobody has to copy an attachment ID.
For completeness: this is not a tool for occasional editors. Anyone able to set up an MCP connection can also find the backend. The gain sits with whoever does the maintenance anyway and wants to turn a press release into a finished post with an image in one go.
My takeaway
The most useful rule from the project is also the shortest: tool calls carry references, not content. A URL instead of the bytes, an attachment ID instead of the image, post_id and permalink instead of the whole post. If a parameter can grow large, put a pointer in it.
That sounds like a detail of image handling, but it is the construction rule of the protocol itself. MCP controls, it does not transport. Accept that, and you get a remarkably clean interface for everything that consists of text anyway — and you save yourself the afternoon spent trying to squeeze a photo through a pipe built for instructions.
The post from that press release is on the site by now, image and credit included. From the first line of code to the published entry took a day — and not a single form.

About the author: Michael Seemann is a senior full-stack developer and frontend architect with 25 years of experience in software development. He currently focuses on AI observability and EU AI Act compliance for enterprise Java applications.
GitHub · Medium · LinkedIn
